Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a standard approach for building LLM applications grounded in private or domain-specific data. However, building a production-ready RAG system requires more than just connecting embeddings to a vector database.
This article outlines the architecture of a session-isolated, scalable RAG system for PDF-based question answering, focusing on design decisions rather than implementation details.
The primary objective is to ensure:
Responses are strictly grounded in retrieved document context, with session-level isolation and scalable ingestion.
System Architecture Overview
The system follows a layered architecture with clear separation of concerns:

• API Layer (request handling)

• Service Layer (business orchestration)

• Repository Layer (data access abstraction)

• Storage Layer (PostgreSQL + Redis + pgvector)

• Async Processing Layer (Celery + RabbitMQ)

• LLM Layer (response generation)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

High-Level Architecture of a Production RAG System for PDF Question AnsweringThe architecture separates real-time inference from asynchronous ingestion workloads.
Core Design Principles
1. Grounded Generation
The LLM operates strictly on retrieved context. No direct knowledge-based answering is allowed.
2. Session Isolation
All data (documents, embeddings, chat history) is scoped per user session, preventing cross-session leakage.
3. Hybrid Retrieval
Instead of relying solely on vector similarity, retrieval combines:

• Semantic similarity (vector search)

• Full-text search ranking

• Keyword overlap scoring
This improves precision for both semantic and lexical queries.

Document Ingestion Pipeline
Document ingestion is designed as an asynchronous pipeline to ensure scalability.
Pipeline Flow

• PDF Upload

• Text Extraction

• PyMuPDF / pdfplumber
3. Text Chunking

• Structured segmentation for embedding efficiency
4. Embedding Generation

• Gemini embeddings or fallback models
5. Vector Storage

• Stored in PostgreSQL with pgvector extension
Asynchronous Execution
To decouple ingestion from API latency:

• Celery workers process ingestion jobs

• RabbitMQ manages task distribution
This ensures ingestion does not block user-facing operations.
Query Execution Flow
When a user submits a query, the system executes the following steps:
1. Query Embedding
The input query is transformed into a vector representation.
2. Hybrid Retrieval
Relevant chunks are retrieved using a weighted scoring model:
score =
0.65 × vector_similarity +
0.25 × text_rank +
0.10 × keyword_match3. Context Assembly
Top-ranked chunks are aggregated into a structured prompt.
4. LLM Generation
The prompt is passed to Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite or any LLM Model, which generates a context-grounded response.
5. Response Delivery
The final output is returned via API.
Memory Architecture
The system uses a dual-layer memory strategy:
Short-Term Memory (Redis)

• Stores recent conversation context

• Optimized for low-latency retrieval
Long-Term Memory (PostgreSQL)

• Persistent chat history

• Session reconstruction capability
This separation ensures both performance and durability.

Layered System Design
API Layer
Responsible for:

• Authentication (JWT)

• Request validation

• Response formatting
No business logic resides here.
Service Layer
Core orchestration layer handling:

• Chat workflows

• Retrieval coordination

• Prompt construction

• Ingestion pipeline management
Repository Layer
Abstracts database operations:

• Document and session management

• Vector search queries

• Chat persistence
This ensures persistence logic remains decoupled from business logic.
Storage Layer

• PostgreSQL: structured data + embeddings

• pgvector: similarity search

• Redis: caching and session memory
Security Model
Security is enforced across multiple layers:

• JWT-based authentication

• bcrypt password hashing

• strict session isolation

• ownership validation for all resources
The system ensures complete data isolation between users.
Scalability Considerations
The architecture is designed for horizontal scalability:

• Stateless API layer

• Async ingestion via Celery

• Distributed message queue (RabbitMQ)

• Vector search optimized with pgvector

• Redis caching for reduced retrieval latency
This allows independent scaling of API and worker layers.
Key Takeaways
This architecture demonstrates how a production-grade RAG system should be designed:

• Separation of ingestion and inference workloads

• Hybrid retrieval for improved accuracy

• Strict session isolation for multi-user safety

• Asynchronous pipelines for scalability

• Layered architecture for maintainability
Rather than relying on a single LLM-centric design, the system is built as a coordinated pipeline of retrieval, ranking, and generation components.
